track.title: Scam Defense
track.description: A practical track for spotting investment red flags before sending money or sharing credentials.

course.title: Scam Defense: Core route
course.description: Slow down suspicious pitches and ask better questions.

lessons.title: Guaranteed returns are a red flag
lessons.summary: Promised high payouts, urgency, and secrecy should make you pause.
lessons.introduction: A suspicious investment pitch often tries to replace questions with excitement. Real products should explain risk, fees, provider status, and withdrawal terms instead of rushing you.
sections: Watch for pressure to send a deposit before you can verify the provider.
Screenshots, celebrity names, or group-chat success stories do not replace public source checks.
A person who avoids questions about regulation, fees, or withdrawals is giving you useful warning information.

scenario.title: Tonight-only deal
scenario.text: A user is told a crypto or forex opportunity closes tonight. They pause, refuse to send money, and ask for public registration details.

lessons.mistake: Mistaking urgency and confidence for evidence.
lessons.action: Write three questions: who regulates it, how withdrawals work, and what can go wrong.
lessons.takeaway: If a pitch punishes careful questions, step back.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify market-player status with official sources such as CMA where relevant.

lessons.title: Recruitment schemes and pyramid risk
lessons.summary: Referral-heavy schemes can hide where money is really coming from.
lessons.introduction: A recruitment-heavy scheme may focus more on bringing new people in than on a clear product or service. If payouts depend mainly on new deposits, the risk to later participants can be severe.
sections: Ask what real asset, service, or regulated activity creates the payout.
Check whether the scheme can explain fees, withdrawals, and provider identity without secrecy.
Be careful when your income depends on recruiting family, classmates, church members, or colleagues.

scenario.title: Campus invite
scenario.text: A student is told to pay KES 2,000 and recruit five friends. They ask where the money comes from and why recruitment matters.

lessons.mistake: Joining because early members appear to have received money.
lessons.action: Map whether money comes from real activity or mainly from new members.
lessons.takeaway: Recruitment pressure is not the same as value creation.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify regulated financial activity with public authorities before sending money.

lessons.title: Fake forex and crypto bots
lessons.summary: Automated trading claims need extra caution, source checks, and credential protection.
lessons.introduction: Forex and crypto bot pitches often use screenshots, lifestyle posts, and technical language. The main safety step is simple: do not send money or credentials before understanding who is behind the offer and what rules apply.
sections: A bot label does not remove market risk or fraud risk.
Do not share exchange logins, seed phrases, OTPs, remote-control access, or bank passwords.
Check whether the person or firm is licensed for the activity they claim to offer.

scenario.title: Telegram bot pitch
scenario.text: A user is asked to deposit KES 15,000 into a bot wallet. They refuse and ask for regulated entity details and withdrawal rules.

lessons.mistake: Trusting a dashboard screenshot without independent verification.
lessons.action: Use the scam checker and list every credential or secret the pitch asks for.
lessons.takeaway: Technology words do not make a pitch safe.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify any capital-market intermediary claims using CMA public sources.

lessons.title: Land pressure sales
lessons.summary: Pressure to pay a land deposit before verification is a serious warning sign.
lessons.introduction: Land scams often exploit urgency, scarcity, family pressure, or fear of missing out. Land due diligence should happen before any deposit or commitment.
sections: Check seller identity, title documents, official search, survey issues, physical access, and legal review.
Do not rely on one broker, one friend, or one screenshot.
Budget for legal, tax, transfer, survey, and time costs before assuming affordability.

scenario.title: Plot closes today
scenario.text: A seller says the plot is available only if a deposit is sent immediately. The user refuses pressure and verifies first.

lessons.mistake: Paying to reserve land before proving the seller can legally sell it.
lessons.action: Create a land verification checklist and do not pay until the checks are complete.
lessons.takeaway: A rushed land deal can become an expensive lesson.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify tax and transfer questions with KRA and qualified professionals; PesaRoute does not give tax or legal advice.

lessons.title: How to use the scam checker
lessons.summary: Paste suspicious pitches and turn red flags into questions.
lessons.introduction: The scam checker is a red-flag tool, not a police report or legal judgment. It helps you slow down and ask better questions before sending money.
sections: Paste the exact pitch without adding private PINs, passwords, or OTPs.
Read the flags and turn each one into a question.
If the amount is material, consider licensed professional review before acting externally.

scenario.title: WhatsApp pitch
scenario.text: A user pastes a message promising fast money. The checker highlights urgency, unclear provider identity, and withdrawal uncertainty.

lessons.mistake: Using the checker after sending money instead of before.
lessons.action: Paste one suspicious pitch and save the questions you would ask.
lessons.takeaway: A red flag is a reason to slow down, not a reason to argue.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Use official public sources for provider checks where applicable.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should you avoid pasting into the scam checker?
quiz_questions.explanation: PesaRoute never asks for PINs, passwords, OTPs, or secrets.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is a warning sign in a recruitment-heavy scheme?
options: Payouts mainly depend on new members
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Payouts mainly depend on new members
quiz_questions.explanation: Referral pressure can hide where money is really coming from.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should happen before a land deposit?
options: Sending money to hold the plot
quiz_questions.explanation: Land verification should come before deposit pressure.

flashcards.back: Slow down and verify before sending money.
The risk that payouts depend mainly on recruiting new participants.
flashcards.example: Pay KES 2,000 then recruit five friends.

flashcards.front: What secrets should never be shared?
flashcards.back: PINs, passwords, OTPs, seed phrases, and remote-control access.
flashcards.example: A bot manager asking for login details is unsafe.

flashcards.front: What is a land pressure red flag?
flashcards.back: A deposit deadline before independent verification.
flashcards.example: Pay today or lose the plot.

flashcards.front: What does the scam checker do?
flashcards.back: It identifies red flags and questions to ask.
flashcards.example: It is not a legal judgment.
